headline: GRAINS DRIFT LOWER, MILK GAINS OVERNIGHT
subheadline: Corn and beans extend Memorial week liquidation while dairy complex catches bid on processing margins.
lead: Corn closed $4.37, down 3 cents for the third straight session of quiet selling that's starting to look methodical. Soybeans held $11.66, off 2 cents, while nearby milk ran to $17.25 on a 3% overnight move higher that caught the complex off guard. The grain liquidation that started Memorial Day is grinding through old-crop positioning without weather premium to stop it.
the_takeaway: Grain liquidation deepens, dairy breaks higher, but volume stays thin.
teaser: Corn extends three-session slide while milk posts biggest overnight move in weeks.

one_number.unit: million barrels crude oil inventory draw
one_number.context: EIA reported the biggest weekly crude draw since March, bringing commercial stocks to five-week lows. Iraq's plan to triple pipeline exports through Kurdistan as Hormuz stays closed is reshaping Middle East crude flows. The inventory tightness is keeping crude supported even as Iran tensions cool from last week's move higher.

yesterdays_call.summary: The liquidation that started Memorial Day isn't selling pressure selling, it's methodical and ongoing.
yesterdays_call.note: Corn and beans both extended the slide exactly as called, no weather premium emerging to stop it.

sections.title: GRAINS GRIND THROUGH SUPPORT
sections.body: Corn lost another 3 cents to $4.37, the third consecutive session below $4.40 and the lowest close since April. December corn gave up 3 cents to $4.63, widening the carry to 26 cents as old-crop liquidation continues. **Deputy Agriculture Secretary Vaden's comments on China honoring soybean purchase commitments** didn't lift beans, which closed $11.66, down 2 cents. The managed money position that built through April is unwinding methodically, no weather stress to interrupt the flow. Belt rainfall overnight was **spotty at best**, not enough to shift the conversation from fund flow to crop conditions.
sections.catalyst: USDA's Vaden comments on China soybean commitments, scattered Belt rainfall
sections.bottom_line: Fund liquidation deepening, China talk not enough to reverse the slide.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: liquidation extended exactly as called, no weather catalyst emerging

sections.title: MILK GAINS ON PROCESSING STRENGTH
sections.body: Class III milk ran to $17.25, up 3% overnight in the session's biggest surprise move. The move higher came on **butter production margins staying north of profitable levels** as Class IV pricing holds firm. Ever.ag's North noted the butter premium is paying well ahead of summer demand patterns. Live cattle eased 50 cents to $238.60, feeders dropped $3.75 to $345.15 as the processing chain stays constrained. The **Cargill Fort Morgan lockout entering its third week** is keeping the cattle complex split between tight processing capacity and steady feed costs.
sections.catalyst: Strong butter margins, ongoing Cargill plant lockout
sections.bottom_line: Dairy breaks higher while cattle processing stays constrained.

sections.title: CRUDE HOLDS IRAN GAINS
sections.body: WTI crude added 85 cents to $95.59, holding most of Monday's Iran-driven move higher as **EIA reported an 8 million barrel inventory draw**. The draw was the biggest since March, bringing commercial stocks to five-week lows. Iraq's plan to triple pipeline exports through Kurdistan is reshaping crude flows as the Strait of Hormuz stays closed to tanker traffic. Natural gas ticked up 2 cents to $3.18, but the energy complex is trading inventory fundamentals more than geopolitical premium now that Iran tensions have cooled from last week's peak.
sections.catalyst: EIA 8 million barrel crude inventory draw, Iraq pipeline expansion
sections.bottom_line: Inventory tightness keeps crude supported as Iran premium fades.

outside_the_pit.title: Data centers competing with cattle for land, water
outside_the_pit.body: Virginia cattle producers say data centers are making land acquisition extremely difficult as tech infrastructure expands across rural areas. The competition for resources is driving up costs and limiting expansion options for livestock operations.

outside_the_pit.title: Trump defends Chinese farmland purchases amid security concerns
outside_the_pit.body: President Trump defended Chinese purchases of U.S. farmland in a Fox News interview, breaking with some Republican calls for stricter foreign ownership limits. The position signals continued focus on agricultural trade relationships over land security restrictions.

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm found 31 miles from US border
outside_the_pit.body: The parasitic fly that eats warm-blooded animals alive was detected just south of the Texas border, raising concerns about potential livestock industry damage. The screwworm could cause millions in economic losses if it crosses into U.S. territory.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: The carry between old crop and new crop corn widened another cent as fund liquidation hits nearby contracts harder than deferred. When the carry rolls this wide in June, it's telling you the market expects no weather premium until pollination stress actually shows up on the radar.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming as river logistics tighten
basis.body: **Eastern Corn Belt basis is adding 2-3 cents** as barge rates firm and ethanol plants restart after maintenance. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal pattern. Soybean basis holding steady with export pace still running behind last year's commitments to China.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain liquidation finish before weather takes control of the story?
weekly_thread.status_text: Liquidation deepened again today, corn breaking $4.40 support with no weather catalyst emerging. Belt rainfall stayed scattered, not enough to shift focus from fund flow to crop stress.

the_more_you_know.title: Why inventory draws matter more than headlines suggest
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 8 million barrel crude draw wasn't just a number, it was information about how Middle East supply chains are actually working. When Iraq triples pipeline exports to offset Hormuz closures, the inventory system shows the strain before prices do. The same logic applies to grain stocks: the monthly USDA numbers tell you where supply really stands versus where the daily headlines say it should be.

watch_list.desc: Weekly export sales; soy under 300K MT keeps chart in charge
Corn below $4.35 tests April lows, weather premium or bust

daily_quote.text: The worst thing about being wrong is the lesson you learn.

source_summary: CME settlement data, EIA petroleum inventory report, USDA Deputy Secretary remarks, weather services
